---
title: "Fabricated Data and the Taxonomy"
date: "2026-07-20"
---

# Fabricated Data and the Taxonomy {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter five-point-four, part three of seven. In part two we saw Petersen's identification strategy: build simulated worlds where he sets the true beta and error structure, then check whether each method's standard error matches the known truth. Now we look at the data, which for a methods paper is unusual — the workhorse dataset is fabricated on purpose. And we meet the taxonomy that the whole paper, and the rest of your empirical life, turns on.
:::

## The Workhorse Data Is Fabricated on Purpose

- For a methods paper, that is a **feature**, not a flaw
- **Simulated panels:** $N$ firms over $T$ years, balanced
- DGP: true slope $\beta$ + firm component + time component + noise
- The three variances are the **knobs** he turns
- Known process $\Rightarrow$ every table reports **true SE** beside estimated SE

::: notes
For a methods paper, the data section is unusual: the workhorse dataset is fabricated on purpose, and that is a feature, not a flaw. The simulated panels are the heart. Each is a balanced panel of N firms over T years, and Petersen explores a range of dimensions to show the conclusions are not knife-edge. A CHECK tag: the exact baseline grid — firms, years, and replications S — should be pulled from his tables; a common configuration is hundreds-to-thousands of firms over roughly ten years, but verify before citing. The data-generating process is transparent: a true slope beta, plus an error built from a firm component, a time component, and idiosyncratic noise — those three variances are the knobs he turns. Because the process is known, every table reports the true standard error beside each method's estimate.
:::

## Real Panels Show the Stakes in the Wild

- Alongside the simulations: **actual** finance regressions
- Same $\hat\beta$, standard errors that swing by a **factor of two**
- Proves the lesson is not an artifact of toy data
- The same regression, very different conclusions about significance

::: notes
Alongside the simulations, Petersen shows the methods on actual finance data, so the reader sees the stakes in the wild — the same beta-hat, standard errors that swing by a factor of two as you change the method. Another CHECK tag in our source: confirm exactly which real datasets and regressions he uses before describing them specifically. The point of pairing real panels with the simulations is to prove the lessons are not an artifact of toy data. On real regressions, the same point estimate carries wildly different conclusions about significance depending on which standard error you report — which is exactly the chaos that motivated the paper in the first place.
:::

## The Taxonomy Is the Real "Data Structure"

| If correlation lives... | it is a | cluster by |
|---|---|---|
| in a firm's residuals over time | firm effect | firm |
| across firms within a period | time effect | time |
| in both directions at once | both | firm **and** time |

- Memorize this table — it is Week Two's recipe

::: notes
But the conceptual payload of this section is the firm-effect versus time-effect distinction itself — the lens through which you classify your own panel before you ever choose a standard error. If the correlation lives in a firm's residuals over time, it is a firm effect, and you cluster by firm. If it lives across firms within a period — a common shock hitting everyone — it is a time effect, and you cluster by time, or use time fixed effects. If it lives in both directions at once, you cluster by firm and time, two-way. Memorize that table. It is Week Two's recipe, and it is the diagnostic you will apply for the rest of your empirical life. Everything else in the paper is in service of proving that this one table is the right way to choose.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The data is **fabricated on purpose** so every table can place the **true SE** beside each method's estimate. Paired with real panels, it shows the same $\hat\beta$ can swing significance by a factor of two. The real payload is the **firm-effect / time-effect / both** taxonomy — your panel diagnostic. Next: reading the tables column by column.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part three: the data is fabricated on purpose, so every table can place the true standard error beside each method's estimate — the comparison that proves the point — and it is paired with real finance panels to show the same beta-hat can swing significance by a factor of two. But the lasting payload is the taxonomy: firm effect, time effect, or both, each mapping to a clustering choice. That is your own panel diagnostic. In part four we put the taxonomy to work and read the simulation tables column by column, watching OLS and White fail and the matching clustering win.
:::
